enrichment.short_description: Anki is a free, open-source spaced-repetition flashcard app that uses active recall to help students, language learners, and medical professionals memorize information efficiently.
enrichment.starting_price: Free (desktop and Android); $24.99 one-time for AnkiMobile on iOS
enrichment.company_size: Small open-source project team; stewardship transitioning to AnkiHub
enrichment.seo_title: Anki Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Anki is the free, open-source spaced-repetition flashcard app trusted by millions of students. See how it works, pricing across platforms, and key features.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Anki is a free, open-source flashcard application built around spaced repetition and active recall, two learning techniques backed by cognitive science research showing they dramatically improve long-term retention. It was created in 2006 by Australian developer Damien Elmes, who originally built it to help himself learn Japanese vocabulary more efficiently. Over nearly two decades, Anki grew from a personal side project into one of the most widely used study tools in the world, with especially deep adoption among medical students (commonly cited as used by a large majority of US medical students), language learners, and anyone preparing for exams that require memorizing large volumes of discrete facts. The core Anki algorithm schedules flashcard reviews at increasing intervals based on how well a user recalls each card, showing difficult cards more often and easy cards less often, which concentrates study time where it is most needed. Anki is not a single monolithic product but an ecosystem: AnkiDesktop is free and open-source on Windows, macOS, and Linux; AnkiDroid is a free, community-built open-source client for Android; AnkiWeb is a free browser-based client and the cloud sync service that keeps decks and review progress consistent across devices; and AnkiMobile is a paid iOS app, sold as a one-time purchase (around $24.99 in the US, with regional Apple App Store pricing elsewhere) rather than a subscription. The iOS app's price is unusual for a mobile app and is explicitly positioned by the developer as the main funding mechanism that supports ongoing development of the entire free ecosystem, since the desktop, Android, and web components generate no direct revenue. Anki supports rich card types (basic, cloze deletion, image occlusion via add-ons), a vast library of user-created decks, and a plugin/add-on system that lets users extend functionality with things like heatmap statistics, custom card templates, and third-party integrations. In February 2026, founder Damien Elmes announced he is gradually transitioning operations and open-source stewardship to AnkiHub, a company founded by Nick Flint and Andrew Sanchez that grew out of the Anki community, while publicly committing to keep the core software open-source, accessible, and free from being degraded for profit. Compared to alternatives like Quizlet, Brainscape, or RemNote, Anki is generally regarded as the most powerful and customizable option for serious long-term learners, at the cost of a steeper learning curve and a more utilitarian interface. It has no ads, no data lock-in (decks are stored in an open, portable format), and a massive community of add-on developers and shared deck creators, making it a go-to recommendation in medical school, language-learning, and competitive-exam communities.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Anki
paragraphs: Anki is a free, open-source flashcard application that uses spaced repetition to help users memorize information more efficiently than traditional study methods. It was created in 2006 by Australian developer Damien Elmes, who built the first version to help himself learn Japanese.

Rather than a single app, Anki is an ecosystem of clients that all sync through AnkiWeb: a free desktop app for Windows, macOS, and Linux; a free, community-built Android app called AnkiDroid; a free web client at AnkiWeb; and a paid iOS app called AnkiMobile that funds the rest of the project.

Anki's name comes from the Japanese word for memorization (an-ki). It has become especially popular in medical education, language learning, and any field that requires memorizing large volumes of discrete facts.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: At its core, Anki uses an algorithm that schedules each flashcard for review at increasing intervals based on how easily the user recalls it, showing difficult cards more frequently and easy cards less often.

Users can create custom card templates, including cloze deletions (fill-in-the-blank cards), image occlusion, and cards with audio or images, making it suitable for subjects ranging from anatomy to vocabulary.

A large add-on ecosystem lets users extend Anki with statistics dashboards, custom review heatmaps, and integrations with other study tools, and a large community of users shares pre-built decks for common subjects like medical boards, language vocabulary, and standardized tests.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Anki is free on desktop (Windows, macOS, Linux) and on Android via AnkiDroid, and free to use on the web through AnkiWeb, which also provides free cloud sync across all devices.
The only paid component is AnkiMobile for iOS, a one-time purchase of around $24.99 in the US with no subscription and no recurring fees; Apple sets regional pricing tiers for other currencies.
The iOS purchase price is explicitly used to fund ongoing development of the entire free ecosystem, since the desktop, Android, and sync components do not generate direct revenue on their own.

features.title: Spaced repetition algorithm
features.description: Schedules card reviews at increasing intervals based on recall difficulty to maximize long-term retention with minimal review time.

features.title: Cross-platform sync via AnkiWeb
features.description: Free cloud sync keeps decks and review history consistent across desktop, Android, iOS, and the web client.

features.title: Custom card types
features.description: Supports basic Q&A cards, cloze deletion, and image occlusion for subjects like anatomy, languages, and exam prep.

features.title: Add-on ecosystem
features.description: A large library of community-built add-ons extends functionality with statistics, custom templates, and third-party integrations.

features.title: Shared decks
features.description: Users can browse and import pre-made decks shared by the Anki community for common subjects and certifications.

features.title: Open, portable data format
features.description: Decks and progress are stored in an open format, so users are never locked into the platform.

features.title: Offline studying
features.description: Desktop and mobile apps work fully offline, syncing progress once a connection is available.

pros: Completely free on desktop, Android, and web with no ads
Highly effective, scientifically grounded spaced-repetition algorithm
Extremely customizable via add-ons and card templates
Huge, active community with a large library of shared decks
Open-source with a portable data format, avoiding vendor lock-in
cons: Steeper learning curve and more utilitarian interface than consumer apps like Quizlet
iOS app requires a one-time $24.99 purchase, unusual for a mobile app
Deck creation and card design require more manual setup than some competitors
Default UI feels dated compared to modern, polished study apps

faqs.question: Is Anki free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Anki is free on Windows, macOS, Linux, and Android, and the AnkiWeb sync service is also free. Only the iOS app, AnkiMobile, requires a one-time purchase.

faqs.question: Why does the iOS app cost money if the rest of Anki is free?
faqs.answer: The AnkiMobile purchase price funds ongoing development of the entire free Anki ecosystem, since the desktop, Android, and web clients do not generate direct revenue.

faqs.question: Who created Anki?
faqs.answer: Anki was created in 2006 by Australian developer Damien Elmes, originally to help him learn Japanese vocabulary.

faqs.question: Does Anki sync across devices?
faqs.answer: Yes, AnkiWeb provides free two-way sync so decks and review progress stay consistent across desktop, mobile, and web.

faqs.question: Is Anki good for medical school?
faqs.answer: Yes, Anki is widely used by medical students, with pre-built shared decks covering common board exam content, and is frequently cited as one of the most popular study tools in medical education.

faqs.question: What is AnkiHub?
faqs.answer: AnkiHub is a company founded by Nick Flint and Andrew Sanchez that grew out of the Anki community; as of early 2026 it is gradually taking over stewardship of Anki's open-source development from creator Damien Elmes.

faqs.question: Can I use Anki offline?
faqs.answer: Yes, the desktop and mobile apps work fully offline and sync progress automatically once you reconnect to the internet.

use_cases.title: Medical school exam preparation
use_cases.description: Medical students use Anki with large shared decks to memorize anatomy, pharmacology, and pathology content ahead of board exams.

use_cases.title: Language vocabulary acquisition
use_cases.description: Language learners build custom decks of vocabulary and grammar patterns, using spaced repetition to retain thousands of words over time.

use_cases.title: General exam and certification study
use_cases.description: Students and professionals preparing for standardized tests or certifications use Anki to retain large volumes of discrete facts efficiently.
use_cases.audience: Students and professionals studying for exams

features: Full flashcard creation and review on iOS

enrichment.short_description: Anomali is an enterprise threat intelligence and agentic SOC platform, built around its ThreatStream product, that helps security teams aggregate, enrich, and act on threat intelligence.
enrichment.seo_title: Anomali Review 2026: Threat Intelligence Platform Guide
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Anomali is an enterprise threat intelligence platform built on ThreatStream. Learn about its history, features, pricing model, and target SOC customers.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Anomali is an American cybersecurity company that provides an enterprise threat intelligence platform used by security operations center (SOC) teams to aggregate, correlate, and act on cyber threat intelligence. The company was founded in 2013 in Redwood City, California, originally under the name ThreatStream, by Greg Martin and Colby DeRodeff. It launched its first threat intelligence platform (TIP) product, also called ThreatStream, that year, designed to ingest threat data from many sources and help analysts track known threats and detect breaches faster. In 2016, the company rebranded from ThreatStream to Anomali while keeping the ThreatStream name for its flagship product. Anomali has raised substantial venture funding over multiple rounds, including a Series B led by General Catalyst Partners and a Series C led by Institutional Venture Partners (IVP), with total funding reported around $96 million by 2018 from investors including Paladin Capital Group, IVP, and GV (formerly Google Ventures). As of 2026, Anomali has approximately 300 employees and continues to operate as a private company headquartered in Redwood City. The core product, ThreatStream (now marketed as ThreatStream Next-Gen), aggregates threat intelligence feeds from open source, commercial, and government sources, applies AI-driven correlation and confidence scoring to reduce noise, and enriches security telemetry, logs, and alerts with context about threat actors, infrastructure, tactics, techniques, and procedures (TTPs), and campaigns in real time. Recent product updates emphasize agentic AI capabilities, including autonomous triage, scoring, and investigation steps intended to accelerate SOC workflows, with Anomali claiming performance improvements of up to 300 times faster investigation compared to traditional manual workflows in enterprise deployments. The platform is designed to integrate with or augment existing security infrastructure such as SIEMs, and can work alongside data lake platforms like Databricks or Snowflake. Anomali targets mid-market to large enterprise SOC teams, threat intelligence analysts, and incident responders rather than individual consumers or small businesses, and pricing is not publicly listed, instead requiring direct engagement with the company's sales team for a custom quote based on data volume, integrations, and deployment scope. It competes with other threat intelligence and SOC platforms such as Recorded Future, ThreatConnect, and Mandiant Advantage. Note: Anomali is unrelated to Anki, the flashcard app, despite the similar name prefix.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Anomali
paragraphs: Anomali is an enterprise cybersecurity company that builds a threat intelligence platform used by security operations center (SOC) teams. It was founded in 2013 as ThreatStream by Greg Martin and Colby DeRodeff, and rebranded to Anomali in 2016 while keeping ThreatStream as the name of its flagship product.
The company is headquartered in Redwood City, California, and has raised roughly $96 million across multiple funding rounds from investors including IVP, Paladin Capital Group, and GV.
Anomali is not related to Anki, the flashcard app; the two names simply share a similar prefix.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: ThreatStream aggregates threat intelligence from open source, commercial, and government feeds into a single platform, giving analysts a consolidated view of known threats, actors, and infrastructure.
The platform applies AI-driven correlation and confidence scoring to threat data, helping reduce alert noise and prioritize the intelligence most relevant to an organization's environment.

Newer 'Next-Gen' capabilities introduce agentic AI features, including autonomous triage and investigation steps designed to accelerate analyst workflows, and the platform is built to integrate with or augment existing SIEM and data lake infrastructure.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Anomali does not publish self-serve pricing on its website. Pricing is custom and quote-based, determined by factors such as data volume, number of integrations, and deployment scale.
Prospective customers must contact Anomali's sales team directly for a quote, which is typical for enterprise-grade threat intelligence and SOC platforms sold to mid-market and large organizations.

features.title: Threat intelligence aggregation
features.description: Consolidates feeds from open source, commercial, and government sources into a unified threat intelligence platform.

features.title: AI-driven correlation and scoring
features.description: Uses AI to correlate threat data and assign confidence scores, reducing noise for analysts.

features.title: Real-time telemetry enrichment
features.description: Applies threat intelligence context to logs, alerts, and events as they occur.

features.title: Agentic SOC automation
features.description: Provides autonomous triage, scoring, and investigation steps to accelerate SOC analyst workflows.

features.title: SIEM and data lake integration
features.description: Integrates with existing SIEM tools or data lake platforms like Databricks and Snowflake.

features.title: Threat actor and campaign context
features.description: Enriches alerts with information about attacker identity, motives, infrastructure, and TTPs.

pros: Long track record in threat intelligence dating back to 2013
Strong enterprise funding and financial backing from major VC firms
AI-driven correlation reduces analyst alert fatigue
Flexible integration with existing SIEM and data lake infrastructure
Purpose-built for SOC and threat intelligence team workflows
cons: No public pricing, requiring a sales engagement to get a quote
Primarily suited to mid-market and enterprise customers, not small teams
Complex platform with a learning curve for new analysts
Value depends heavily on quality of integrated data sources

faqs.question: What is Anomali used for?
faqs.answer: Anomali is a threat intelligence platform used by SOC teams to aggregate, correlate, and act on cyber threat intelligence, enriching alerts and telemetry with context on threat actors and campaigns.

faqs.question: When was Anomali founded?
faqs.answer: Anomali was founded in 2013 as ThreatStream by Greg Martin and Colby DeRodeff, and rebranded to Anomali in 2016.

faqs.question: Is Anomali the same as Anki?
faqs.answer: No, Anomali and Anki are unrelated products. Anki is a free flashcard app for studying, while Anomali is an enterprise cybersecurity threat intelligence platform.

faqs.question: How much does Anomali cost?
faqs.answer: Anomali does not publish pricing publicly. Pricing is custom and based on factors like data volume and deployment scope, requiring a quote from sales.

faqs.question: Who are Anomali's typical customers?
faqs.answer: Anomali targets mid-market and enterprise security operations center (SOC) teams, threat intelligence analysts, and incident responders.

faqs.question: What is ThreatStream?
faqs.answer: ThreatStream is Anomali's flagship threat intelligence platform product, originally the company's name before it rebranded to Anomali in 2016.

faqs.question: Where is Anomali headquartered?
faqs.answer: Anomali is headquartered in Redwood City, California.

enrichment.short_description: anon.li Alias is a privacy-focused email aliasing service that lets users generate disposable or custom email addresses to protect their real inbox from spam and data leaks.
enrichment.seo_title: anon.li Alias Review 2026: Email Aliasing Pricing and Features
enrichment.seo_meta_description: anon.li Alias is an open-source email aliasing tool for creating disposable and custom addresses. See features, pricing, and how it protects your inbox.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: anon.li Alias is the email aliasing product of anon.li, a privacy-focused, fully open-source platform based in Liechtenstein that also offers end-to-end encrypted file sharing (called Drop) and forms. Alias lets users generate a unique email identity for every website or service they sign up for, rather than exposing their real email address. Aliases can be random strings or custom, human-readable names (for example, shop@anon.li), and messages sent to an alias are forwarded to the user's real inbox; users can also reply through the alias without revealing their actual address. The product supports management through the anon.li website, a command-line interface (CLI), an API, a browser extension (available for Chrome and Firefox), and even an MCP (Model Context Protocol) integration aimed at AI agents that need to generate throwaway identities programmatically. Optional PGP encryption is available for forwarded mail on paid tiers. anon.li operates on a freemium model: the free tier includes a limited number of aliases (reported as around 10 random aliases plus 1 custom alias), while the paid Alias plan, priced at roughly $2.49 per month, unlocks unlimited random aliases, a larger allotment of custom aliases (around 100), and support for custom domains. anon.li also bundles Alias with its Drop file-sharing product at a combined price point for users who want both services. The entire codebase is open source under the GNU Affero General Public License v3.0 (AGPLv3) and published on both GitHub and Codeberg, reflecting a 'privacy by default, not by request' philosophy and zero-knowledge architecture where the platform itself cannot read forwarded content or shared files. Because the project is small, independent, and privacy-first, detailed public information about its founding date, team size, or funding is not available; it presents itself as a lean, community-oriented alternative to larger email alias and privacy services like SimpleLogin, AnonAddy (addy.io), or Firefox Relay, differentiating primarily through its open-source transparency, CLI/API/MCP access, and jurisdiction in privacy-friendly Liechtenstein. It is best suited for privacy-conscious individuals, developers, and increasingly AI agent workflows that need to generate disposable identities without manual sign-up steps.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is anon.li Alias
paragraphs: anon.li Alias is an email aliasing feature of the broader anon.li privacy platform, which is based in Liechtenstein and fully open source. It lets users create disposable or custom email addresses that forward to their real inbox, keeping their actual email address hidden from the services they sign up for.
anon.li positions itself around a 'privacy by default, not by request' philosophy, and Alias is one of three core products alongside Drop (end-to-end encrypted file sharing) and Forms.
Because it is a small, independent, open-source project, detailed corporate information such as founding year or team size is not publicly disclosed.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Users can generate random aliases or choose custom, memorable addresses (like shop@anon.li) for each service they sign up for, reducing spam exposure and making it easy to identify which company leaked or sold an email address.

Alias supports management through multiple interfaces: the anon.li website, a CLI, a REST API, a browser extension, and an MCP integration that lets AI agents generate and manage aliases programmatically.
Users can reply to forwarded messages directly through their alias without exposing their real email address, and paid tiers add optional PGP encryption and custom domain support.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: anon.li offers a free tier that includes a limited number of aliases, reported as around 10 random aliases and 1 custom alias.
The paid Alias plan costs roughly $2.49 per month and unlocks unlimited random aliases, around 100 custom aliases, and custom domain support.
A bundle plan combining Alias with anon.li's Drop file-sharing product is also available at a combined monthly price.

features.description: Generate throwaway random addresses or memorable custom aliases for each service you sign up for.

features.description: Create aliases on the fly while signing up for services, available for Chrome and Firefox.

features.title: Optional PGP encryption
features.description: Adds end-to-end encryption for forwarded mail on paid plans.

features.title: Custom domain support
features.description: Paid plans allow using your own domain for aliases.

pros: Fully open source and self-auditable codebase under AGPLv3
Low-cost paid tier compared to many privacy email services
Multiple access methods including CLI, API, and browser extension
Privacy-friendly jurisdiction in Liechtenstein
Unique MCP support for AI agent workflows
cons: Free tier is limited to a small number of aliases
Limited public information about the company or team behind it
Smaller brand recognition than established alias services like SimpleLogin or Firefox Relay
Custom domain support requires a paid plan

faqs.question: What is anon.li Alias?
faqs.answer: anon.li Alias is an email aliasing service that lets users create random or custom email addresses that forward to their real inbox, protecting their actual address from spam and data leaks.

faqs.question: Is anon.li Alias free?
faqs.answer: Yes, there is a free tier with a limited number of aliases (around 10 random and 1 custom). A paid plan starting around $2.49 per month unlocks unlimited random aliases and more custom aliases.

faqs.question: Is anon.li open source?
faqs.answer: Yes, anon.li is fully open source under the AGPLv3 license, with code published on GitHub and Codeberg.

faqs.question: Where is anon.li based?
faqs.answer: anon.li operates under Liechtenstein jurisdiction, which is known for strong privacy protections.

faqs.question: Can I reply to emails through my alias?
faqs.answer: Yes, anon.li Alias supports replying to forwarded messages through the alias itself without exposing your real email address.

faqs.question: Does anon.li Alias support custom domains?
faqs.answer: Yes, custom domain support is available on paid plans.

faqs.question: Can AI agents use anon.li Alias?
faqs.answer: Yes, anon.li offers an MCP (Model Context Protocol) integration that lets AI agents generate and manage aliases programmatically.

use_cases.title: Tracking data leaks
use_cases.description: Use custom aliases per service to identify which company leaked or sold your email address if spam starts arriving.

use_cases.title: Automated alias generation for AI agents
use_cases.description: Developers use the API, CLI, or MCP integration to programmatically generate disposable email identities for automated workflows.

enrichment.short_description: Antideo is an IP intelligence and fraud prevention API that helps businesses detect proxies, VPNs, and fraud risk signals while validating emails and phone numbers.
enrichment.seo_title: Antideo Review 2026: Fraud Prevention API Pricing and Features
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Antideo is an IP intelligence and fraud prevention API for detecting proxies, VPNs, and fraud risk. Compare pricing plans, features, and use cases.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Antideo is a fraud prevention and data validation API platform built by Bizmindz Technologies LLP, a company based in Kerala, India. The product provides a suite of APIs centered on IP intelligence: IP geolocation, IP reputation scoring, and detection of VPNs, proxies, and other anonymization techniques that fraudsters use to disguise their true location and identity. Alongside IP-focused tools, Antideo offers email validation (identifying disposable, temporary, and invalid email addresses) and phone number validation (checking national and international numbers for validity and risk), giving businesses a combined toolkit for filtering out fake signups, spam form submissions, and fraudulent transactions before they cause damage. The company positions Antideo as a cost-effective alternative to larger, more expensive fraud and risk API providers such as IPQualityScore and MaxMind, emphasizing that it helps businesses 'save thousands of dollars' annually by keeping prospect lists and signup funnels clean and protecting sender domain reputation from being harmed by spam or invalid emails. Antideo's pricing follows a freemium, usage-based structure: a free tier offers 10 API calls per hour with no credit card required, a Starter tier costs around $5 per month for 50 requests per hour, and an Enterprise tier costs around $80 per month for 2,000 requests per hour, with the ability to scale further for high-volume customers. The company reports serving over 240,000 API calls daily across more than 100 client companies globally, spanning industries that need to screen users at signup, checkout, or lead-capture stages. Antideo also offers plugins for platforms like WordPress to make integration easier for non-technical users. Its target customers are primarily small and mid-sized businesses, SaaS companies, e-commerce platforms, and marketing teams that need an affordable, developer-friendly way to add basic fraud and risk signals to their sign-up or checkout flows, rather than large enterprises with dedicated fraud teams who might instead choose more comprehensive platforms. No public information is available about Antideo's founding year, funding, or headcount, consistent with it being a smaller, self-funded or bootstrapped product.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Antideo
paragraphs: Antideo is a fraud prevention and data validation API built by Bizmindz Technologies LLP, based in Kerala, India. It centers on IP intelligence, providing geolocation, reputation scoring, and VPN/proxy detection to help businesses identify fraudulent or high-risk users.
Beyond IP data, Antideo also validates email addresses (flagging disposable or invalid addresses) and phone numbers, giving businesses a combined toolkit to screen signups, leads, and transactions.
The company markets itself as a lower-cost alternative to larger fraud-detection API providers, targeting businesses that want essential risk signals without enterprise-level pricing.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: IP reputation and geolocation APIs help identify the true location and risk level of an incoming connection, flagging known bad actors and suspicious IP ranges.
VPN, proxy, and Tor detection identifies users attempting to mask their real location or identity, a common signal used in fraud prevention and account abuse detection.
Email and phone validation APIs check for disposable, temporary, or invalid contact details, helping businesses keep marketing and signup data clean.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Antideo offers a free tier with 10 API calls per hour and no credit card required, suitable for testing and very low-volume use.
The Starter plan costs around $5 per month for 50 requests per hour, while the Enterprise plan costs around $80 per month for 2,000 requests per hour.
Pricing scales with API call volume, and the company states it serves over 240,000 API calls daily across its customer base.

features.description: Simplifies adding fraud and validation checks to WordPress-based signup and contact forms.

Free tier available with no credit card required

Simple integration options including a WordPress plugin
Reports high daily API call volume, suggesting production reliability
cons: Limited public information about company founding, funding, or team size
Smaller brand recognition than established players like MaxMind or IPQualityScore
Advanced enterprise fraud features may be less mature than larger competitors
Detailed documentation and case studies are less extensive than bigger providers

faqs.question: What does Antideo do?
faqs.answer: Antideo provides APIs for IP intelligence, VPN and proxy detection, email validation, and phone number validation to help businesses prevent fraud and clean up signup data.

faqs.question: Is Antideo free?
faqs.answer: Antideo offers a free tier with 10 API calls per hour and no credit card required. Paid plans start around $5 per month for higher volume.

faqs.question: Who owns Antideo?
faqs.answer: Antideo is operated by Bizmindz Technologies LLP, a company based in Kerala, India.

faqs.question: What is Antideo's Enterprise plan?
faqs.answer: The Enterprise plan costs around $80 per month and includes 2,000 API requests per hour.

faqs.question: Can Antideo detect VPNs and proxies?
faqs.answer: Yes, Antideo's IP intelligence API is designed to detect VPNs, proxies, and other anonymization techniques used by fraudsters.

faqs.question: Does Antideo validate email addresses?
faqs.answer: Yes, Antideo offers an email validation API that flags disposable, temporary, and invalid addresses.

faqs.question: Who uses Antideo?
faqs.answer: Antideo is used by small and mid-sized businesses, SaaS companies, and e-commerce platforms that need affordable fraud and data validation checks.

enrichment.short_description: Anvil is a Python-based platform for building full-stack web applications, including the frontend, using only Python and no HTML, CSS, or JavaScript.
enrichment.company_size: small team based in Cambridge, UK
enrichment.seo_title: Anvil.works Review 2026: Python Web App Builder Pricing
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Anvil.works lets you build full-stack web apps with only Python. Compare pricing plans, features, and use cases for this no-code Python platform.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Anvil, at anvil.works, is a web application development platform that lets developers build complete full-stack web apps entirely in Python, without writing HTML, CSS, or JavaScript for the frontend. It was created by Meredydd Luff and Ian Davies, who met while completing PhDs at the University of Cambridge Computer Laboratory; Luff serves as CEO and Davies as COO, and the company operates from Cambridge, UK. The first working prototype of Anvil's development environment launched in 2017. Anvil's core differentiator is a drag-and-drop visual designer for building the user interface, paired with a Python code editor for both client-side (in-browser, compiled to run via Skulpt/similar Python-in-browser tooling) and server-side logic, plus a built-in relational database (Data Tables) and one-click cloud hosting, all managed from a single browser-based IDE. This lets Python developers, including those without web development (HTML/CSS/JavaScript) experience, build and deploy interactive web apps, internal tools, and dashboards much faster than assembling a traditional stack of separate frontend and backend frameworks. Anvil supports installing external Python libraries (including many PyPI packages) for use in both client and server code, integrates with Git for version control and CI/CD-style workflows, and offers features like scheduled background tasks, custom domains, and single sign-on (SSO) on higher tiers. Pricing follows a freemium SaaS model: a Free plan offers unlimited apps with shared cloud hosting, limited database rows (around 50,000) and storage (100 MB); a Hobby plan at roughly $12 per month (billed annually, or $14 month-to-month) adds scheduled tasks, a custom domain, and higher limits; a Business plan at roughly $89 per month (annual) or $99 (monthly) adds team collaboration, SSO, staging environments, and much higher data limits; and an Enterprise tier offers custom pricing with on-premise or dedicated-server deployment, isolated databases, and corporate authentication integration. Anvil also offers free educational licenses for students and educators. Its target audience includes Python developers building internal business tools, startups building MVPs quickly, data teams building dashboards on top of Python data science libraries, and educators teaching programming, and it competes with other low-code/no-code and rapid app-building platforms such as Streamlit, Retool, and Django-based scaffolding tools, differentiating primarily through its all-in-one, pure-Python, drag-and-drop approach to full-stack development.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Anvil.works
paragraphs: Anvil is a platform for building full-stack web applications using only Python, including the user interface, eliminating the need to write separate HTML, CSS, or JavaScript. It was founded by Meredydd Luff and Ian Davies, who met at the University of Cambridge Computer Laboratory, with the first prototype launching in 2017.

The platform combines a drag-and-drop visual designer for building interfaces with a Python code editor for logic, a built-in relational database called Data Tables, and one-click cloud hosting, all inside a single browser-based development environment.
Anvil is aimed at Python developers, including those without traditional web development experience, who want to build and deploy interactive web apps and internal tools quickly.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: The visual, drag-and-drop UI builder lets users design app interfaces without writing frontend code, while still allowing full Python logic to control behavior.
Data Tables provide a built-in relational database accessible directly from Python code, removing the need to set up and manage a separate database service for many use cases.
Anvil supports installing external Python packages, integrates with Git, and includes features like scheduled background tasks, custom domains, and SSO on paid tiers.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Anvil offers a Free plan with unlimited apps, shared cloud hosting, roughly 50,000 database rows, and 100 MB storage.
The Hobby plan costs about $12 per month billed annually (or $14 month-to-month) and adds scheduled tasks, a custom domain, and higher storage and row limits.

The Business plan costs about $89 per month billed annually (or $99 month-to-month) and adds team collaboration, SSO, staging environments, and much higher data limits, while Enterprise pricing is custom and includes on-premise deployment options.

features.description: A managed relational database accessible directly from Python without separate database setup.

cons: Free and lower tiers use shared hosting, which may limit performance

Less flexible than hand-coded frontends for highly custom UI/UX needs
Smaller ecosystem and community compared to mainstream web frameworks

faqs.question: What is Anvil.works?
faqs.answer: Anvil is a platform that lets developers build full-stack web applications entirely in Python, including the user interface, without writing HTML, CSS, or JavaScript.

faqs.question: Who created Anvil?
faqs.answer: Anvil was created by Meredydd Luff and Ian Davies, who met at the University of Cambridge Computer Laboratory, with the first prototype launching in 2017.

faqs.question: Is Anvil free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, Anvil offers a free plan with unlimited apps, shared hosting, and limited database rows and storage. Paid plans start around $12 per month.

faqs.question: Does Anvil require knowledge of HTML or JavaScript?
faqs.answer: No, Anvil's visual designer and Python-based logic mean users do not need to write HTML, CSS, or JavaScript to build a functional web app.

faqs.question: Can Anvil apps use external Python libraries?
faqs.answer: Yes, Anvil supports installing many external Python packages for use in both client and server-side code.

faqs.question: Does Anvil support team collaboration?
faqs.answer: Yes, team collaboration features are available starting on the Business plan, along with SSO and staging environments.

faqs.question: Is there a free option for students?
faqs.answer: Yes, Anvil offers free educational licenses for students and educators.

enrichment.short_description: AnyDesk is a remote desktop software platform that provides fast, low-latency remote access and support across Windows, macOS, Linux, iOS, and Android, competing directly with TeamViewer.
enrichment.seo_title: AnyDesk Review 2026: Pricing Plans and Key Features
enrichment.seo_meta_description: AnyDesk is a fast, cross-platform remote desktop tool. Compare its Solo, Standard, Advanced, and Ultimate pricing plans, features, and free version.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: AnyDesk is a remote desktop and remote support software platform developed by AnyDesk Software GmbH, a company founded in 2014 and headquartered in Stuttgart, Germany. The software allows users to remotely access and control computers and mobile devices over the internet, and is widely positioned as a faster, more cost-effective alternative to the market leader TeamViewer. AnyDesk's core technical differentiator is its proprietary video codec, DeskRT, which the company markets as delivering very low latency and high frame rates even over limited bandwidth connections, making remote sessions feel closer to working directly on the local machine. As of recent company reporting, AnyDesk has surpassed 500 million downloads and serves over 100,000 business customers worldwide, spanning IT support teams, managed service providers (MSPs), enterprises needing unattended server access, and individual users needing occasional remote access to their own devices. AnyDesk is free for personal, non-commercial use, offering basic remote access on a single device without advanced features like file transfer, remote printing, session recording, or unattended access. For professional and business use, AnyDesk offers four paid license tiers, all billed annually with no monthly-only option: Solo (around $14.90/month equivalent, single concurrent session, for individual professionals), Standard (around $49.90/month equivalent, 1 to 20 concurrent connections, for small teams), Advanced (around $111.90/month equivalent, 2 to 50 concurrent connections, aimed at IT departments and MSPs), and Ultimate (custom enterprise pricing with unlimited concurrent connections, aimed at large organizations with advanced security, deployment, and management needs). Under AnyDesk's licensing model, only the session initiator needs a paid license, while the person being connected to (the participant) can join for free, which is a common draw for support scenarios. Paid tiers unlock features like unattended access, file transfer, remote printing, session recording, address book management, and more granular access permissions, with higher tiers adding features like custom branding, session policies, and centralized device management for larger IT teams. AnyDesk is cross-platform, supporting Windows, macOS, Linux, iOS, Android, and Raspberry Pi, among other platforms, and is used across a very broad range of scenarios from personal tech support to enterprise IT helpdesk and unattended server administration.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is AnyDesk
paragraphs: AnyDesk is a remote desktop software platform developed by AnyDesk Software GmbH, founded in 2014 and headquartered in Stuttgart, Germany. It lets users remotely access and control computers and devices over the internet, positioning itself as a fast, cost-effective alternative to TeamViewer.
The platform is built around a proprietary video codec called DeskRT, designed to deliver low-latency, high-frame-rate remote sessions even on limited bandwidth connections.
AnyDesk has reported over 500 million downloads and more than 100,000 business customers worldwide, spanning individual users, IT support teams, and large enterprises.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: AnyDesk supports cross-platform remote access across Windows, macOS, Linux, iOS, Android, and other platforms, letting users connect between different operating systems seamlessly.
Paid tiers add unattended access (connecting to a device without someone present to accept), file transfer, remote printing, and session recording for support and compliance purposes.

Higher tiers add centralized device management, custom branding, granular session policies, and support for larger numbers of concurrent connections, aimed at IT departments and managed service providers.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: AnyDesk is free for personal, non-commercial use, offering basic remote access on a single device but without advanced features like unattended access or file transfer.

Paid licenses are billed annually and come in four tiers: Solo (around $14.90/month equivalent) for individuals, Standard (around $49.90/month equivalent) for small teams with 1 to 20 concurrent connections, Advanced (around $111.90/month equivalent) for IT teams with 2 to 50 concurrent connections, and Ultimate with custom pricing for unlimited concurrent connections.
Only the person initiating a remote session needs a paid license; the person being connected to can join a session for free.

Pricing can escalate quickly for teams needing many concurrent connections

faqs.question: Is AnyDesk free?
faqs.answer: Yes, AnyDesk is free for personal, non-commercial use with basic remote access, but lacks advanced features like unattended access and file transfer, which require a paid license.

faqs.question: Who owns AnyDesk?
faqs.answer: AnyDesk is developed by AnyDesk Software GmbH, founded in 2014 and headquartered in Stuttgart, Germany.

faqs.question: How is AnyDesk priced for businesses?
faqs.answer: AnyDesk offers four paid tiers billed annually: Solo, Standard, Advanced, and Ultimate, priced based on the number of concurrent connections needed.

faqs.question: Does the person receiving a remote connection need a license?
faqs.answer: No, only the person initiating the remote session needs a paid AnyDesk license. The participant can join for free.

faqs.question: What makes AnyDesk fast?
faqs.answer: AnyDesk uses a proprietary video codec called DeskRT, designed to minimize latency and maintain high frame rates even over limited bandwidth.

faqs.question: Is AnyDesk available on mobile?
faqs.answer: Yes, AnyDesk supports iOS and Android in addition to Windows, macOS, and Linux.

faqs.question: How does AnyDesk compare to TeamViewer?
faqs.answer: AnyDesk is commonly positioned as a faster, more cost-effective alternative to TeamViewer, particularly for latency-sensitive remote sessions.

enrichment.short_description: AnySummary is an AI-powered summarization tool that condenses text, PDFs, audio, and video files into concise summaries in customizable formats.
enrichment.seo_title: AnySummary Review 2026: AI Summarizer Pricing and Features
enrichment.seo_meta_description: AnySummary uses AI to summarize text, PDFs, audio, and video into concise formats. Compare free vs paid plans, features, and supported file types.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: AnySummary is an AI-powered summarization tool that uses large language model (LLM) and natural language processing (NLP) technology to condense long-form content into concise, digestible summaries. It supports a wide range of input formats, including .pdf, .docx, .jpg, .png, .mov, .pptx, .mp3, .mp4, .txt, .wav, .csv, and .m4a files, as well as the ability to summarize content directly from a URL, meaning it can handle documents, presentations, images, audio recordings, and video files rather than only plain text. Users can customize the output format of a summary, choosing between bullet points, direct quotes, or a full narrative abstract, and can supply their own custom instructions to guide what the AI focuses on, or let the AI determine the most relevant points automatically. On the data handling side, AnySummary states that uploaded files are stored on secure servers and automatically deleted 24 hours after processing, with industry-standard encryption used to protect user data in the interim. Pricing follows a freemium model: the Free plan allows a limited number of file summaries per day (commonly cited as around 5 files per day) with basic feature access, while the paid Plus (or Pro) plan is priced in the range of $7 to $15 per month depending on the source, and unlocks a much higher daily file limit (around 50 files per day in some reporting), access to full transcripts, and faster processing speeds. The target audience for AnySummary spans students, researchers, content marketers, business professionals, journalists, educators, freelancers, small teams, and non-native English speakers, essentially anyone who regularly needs to process large volumes of written or spoken content quickly. Public information about the company behind AnySummary, including its founding date, headquarters, or team size, is limited; third-party review sites list the tool as having launched around September 2025 and note that its profile on some directories remains unclaimed by the original creators, suggesting it is a smaller, likely bootstrapped product rather than a venture-backed company. It competes in a crowded field of AI summarization tools alongside products like Recall, Eightify, and various ChatGPT-based summarizer wrappers, differentiating primarily through its broad multi-format file support (including audio and video) and flexible summary output styles.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is AnySummary
paragraphs: AnySummary is an AI-powered summarization tool that uses large language model technology to condense text, documents, images, audio, and video into concise summaries. It supports a wide range of file types, including PDFs, Word documents, PowerPoint files, images, MP3 and WAV audio, and MP4 and MOV video, as well as summarizing content directly from a URL.
Users can choose how their summary is formatted, selecting bullet points, quotes, or a full narrative abstract, and can provide custom instructions to steer what the AI focuses on.
Public information about the company behind AnySummary is limited, with the tool appearing to be a smaller, independently run product rather than a large venture-backed company.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: AnySummary supports summarizing a broad range of file formats, including PDFs, Word documents, images, presentations, and audio and video files, rather than being limited to plain text.
Users can customize the output format of a summary between bullet points, quotes, or a full abstract, and can supply their own summarization instructions.
The platform automatically deletes uploaded files 24 hours after processing and uses industry-standard encryption to protect user data during that window.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: AnySummary offers a Free plan that allows a limited number of file summaries per day, commonly cited as around 5 files daily, with basic summarization features.
The paid Plus plan is priced in the range of $7 to $15 per month depending on the source, unlocking a significantly higher daily file limit, access to full transcripts, and faster processing.
There is no publicly listed enterprise or team pricing tier beyond the individual Free and Plus plans.

faqs.question: What file types does AnySummary support?
faqs.answer: AnySummary supports PDF, DOCX, JPG, PNG, MOV, PPTX, MP3, MP4, TXT, WAV, CSV, and M4A files, as well as summarizing content directly from a URL.

faqs.question: Is AnySummary free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, AnySummary offers a free plan with a limited number of daily file summaries. A paid Plus plan removes most limits and adds features like full transcripts.

faqs.question: How much does AnySummary cost?
faqs.answer: The paid plan is priced in the range of $7 to $15 per month depending on the source, offering a higher daily file limit and faster processing than the free plan.

faqs.question: Does AnySummary keep my uploaded files?
faqs.answer: No, AnySummary states that uploaded files are automatically deleted from its servers 24 hours after processing.

faqs.question: Can AnySummary summarize audio and video?
faqs.answer: Yes, AnySummary supports summarizing audio files (MP3, WAV, M4A) and video files (MP4, MOV) in addition to documents and images.

faqs.question: Can I customize the summary format?
faqs.answer: Yes, users can choose between bullet points, quotes, or a full narrative abstract, and can provide custom instructions for the AI.

faqs.question: Who is AnySummary designed for?
faqs.answer: AnySummary targets students, researchers, content marketers, journalists, educators, and business professionals who need to process large volumes of content quickly.

enrichment.short_description: Apache NetBeans is a free, open-source integrated development environment (IDE) for Java and other languages, now governed as a top-level Apache Software Foundation project.
enrichment.seo_title: Apache NetBeans Review 2026: Free Java IDE Guide
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Apache NetBeans is a free, open-source IDE for Java, PHP, C/C++, and more. Learn its history from Sun to Oracle to Apache, features, and current status.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Apache NetBeans is a free, open-source integrated development environment (IDE) primarily used for Java development, though it also supports PHP, C, C++, HTML5, JavaScript, Groovy, and other languages through extensions. Its origins trace back to 1996, when it began as Xelfi (a wordplay on Delphi), a student Java IDE project at the Faculty of Mathematics and Physics at Charles University in Prague. In 1997, Roman Staněk formed a company around the project and produced commercial versions until it was acquired by Sun Microsystems in 1999. Sun open-sourced NetBeans the following year, and the name itself, coined by architect Jaroslav Tulach, derives from Network and Java Beans. When Oracle acquired Sun Microsystems in 2010, NetBeans became an Oracle-owned product; under Oracle, the codebase was eventually unified with Oracle's own JDeveloper IDE around a shared NetBeans Platform architecture by 2012. In 2016, Oracle donated the NetBeans source code to the Apache Software Foundation, where it entered the Apache Incubator as a podling; the project graduated to become a full Top-Level Project of the Apache Software Foundation in April 2019, giving it the current name Apache NetBeans and community-driven, vendor-neutral governance. As of 2026, Apache NetBeans continues to be actively developed under this governance model, with Apache NetBeans 30 released in May 2026, reflecting a steady release cadence. The IDE provides a full suite of development tools including a code editor with syntax highlighting and code completion, visual GUI builders, debugging tools, profiling tools, version control integration (Git, Subversion, Mercurial), and project management features, all delivered as a completely free download with no licensing fees, tiers, or paid upgrades, consistent with its status as a fully open-source Apache Software Foundation project. Because it is community-governed rather than commercially operated, there is no company size, headquarters, or founding company in the traditional sense; contributions come from a distributed, volunteer-driven open-source community following Apache Software Foundation governance norms. Apache NetBeans is commonly compared to other free and open-source IDEs like Eclipse and VS Code, and to JetBrains' commercial IntelliJ IDEA, competing primarily on being a lightweight, fully free, and long-established option for Java development with strong out-of-the-box support for standard Java application types including Java SE, Java EE, and JavaFX.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Apache Netbeans
paragraphs: Apache NetBeans is a free, open-source integrated development environment (IDE) primarily known for Java development, though it also supports languages like PHP, C, C++, HTML5, and JavaScript through extensions.

The project traces back to 1996 as a student project called Xelfi at Charles University in Prague, was commercialized in 1997, acquired by Sun Microsystems in 1999, and open-sourced shortly after. It passed to Oracle when Oracle acquired Sun in 2010.

In 2016, Oracle donated the NetBeans codebase to the Apache Software Foundation, and the project became a full Top-Level Apache project in April 2019, giving it its current name and community governance model.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: The IDE provides a full-featured code editor with syntax highlighting, code completion, and refactoring tools for Java and other supported languages.
NetBeans includes out-of-the-box support for building Java SE, Java EE, JavaFX, and mobile Java applications, along with visual GUI builder tools for designing desktop application interfaces.
Built-in debugging and profiling tools, along with integration for version control systems like Git, Subversion, and Mercurial, support a complete development workflow within a single IDE.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Apache NetBeans is completely free to download and use, with no licensing fees, paid tiers, or premium upgrades, consistent with its status as an Apache Software Foundation open-source project.
There is no commercial entity behind Apache NetBeans; development is community-driven and governed by Apache Software Foundation open-source norms.

features.title: Multi-language support
features.description: Supports Java, PHP, C, C++, HTML5, JavaScript, and Groovy development through built-in tools and extensions.

features.title: Java SE, EE, and JavaFX support
features.description: Provides out-of-the-box tooling for all major Java application types, including desktop, web, and enterprise apps.

features.title: Visual GUI builder
features.description: Includes drag-and-drop tools for designing Java Swing and JavaFX desktop application interfaces.

features.title: Integrated debugger and profiler
features.description: Built-in tools for debugging code and profiling application performance without external plugins.

features.title: Version control integration
features.description: Native support for Git, Subversion, and Mercurial version control systems.

features.title: Code completion and refactoring
features.description: Smart code completion, error highlighting, and refactoring tools speed up development.

features.description: Runs on Windows, macOS, and Linux, consistent with its Java-based architecture.

pros: Completely free and open-source with no licensing costs
Strong out-of-the-box support for Java SE, EE, and JavaFX
Long development history dating back to the late 1990s with continued active releases
Vendor-neutral governance under the Apache Software Foundation
Built-in GUI builder, debugger, and profiler reduce need for external tools
cons: Smaller plugin ecosystem compared to Eclipse or VS Code
Less market share among professional Java developers than JetBrains' IntelliJ IDEA
Interface can feel dated compared to more modern IDEs
Community-driven development can mean slower feature turnaround than commercial competitors

faqs.question: Is Apache NetBeans free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Apache NetBeans is completely free and open-source, with no licensing fees or paid tiers, as it is governed by the Apache Software Foundation.

faqs.question: What languages does Apache NetBeans support?
faqs.answer: Apache NetBeans primarily supports Java (SE, EE, and JavaFX) but also supports PHP, C, C++, HTML5, JavaScript, and Groovy through built-in tools and extensions.

faqs.question: Who owns NetBeans now?
faqs.answer: NetBeans is not owned by a single company. It was donated by Oracle to the Apache Software Foundation in 2016 and became a Top-Level Apache project in April 2019, now governed by a community of volunteer contributors.

faqs.question: How old is NetBeans?
faqs.answer: NetBeans traces its origins to 1996 as a student project called Xelfi at Charles University in Prague, making it one of the longer-running Java IDEs still in active development.

faqs.question: Was NetBeans previously owned by Oracle?
faqs.answer: Yes, NetBeans became part of Oracle when Oracle acquired Sun Microsystems in 2010, before Oracle donated the codebase to the Apache Software Foundation in 2016.

faqs.question: What is the latest version of Apache NetBeans?
faqs.answer: Apache NetBeans 30 was released in May 2026, reflecting the project's ongoing active development under the Apache Software Foundation.

faqs.question: How does Apache NetBeans compare to Eclipse?
faqs.answer: Both are free, open-source Java IDEs, but NetBeans is known for strong out-of-the-box support for Java SE, EE, and JavaFX with less reliance on plugins, while Eclipse has a larger third-party plugin ecosystem.
